title: Chapter 15: The Journalist
content: The Byline didn't have a sign. Just a painted window that said BAR in fading gold letters above a door that stuck in wet weather and swung too freely in dry. Downtown Oakland, three blocks from the Tribune's old headquarters — the building was condos now, but the bar remembered what the neighborhood used to be.

Chloe pushed through the door and the smell hit Maya like a textbook — stale beer soaked into floorboards over decades, layered under the sharper tang of lemon cleaner that had given up somewhere around 2019. Beneath it all, old newsprint. The walls were papered in it: framed front pages going back fifty years, press passes laminated and pinned in clusters like medals on a general's chest. WAR ENDS. NIXON RESIGNS. BERLIN WALL FALLS. A yellowed copy of the Oakland Tribune from 1989, the Loma Prieta earthquake, the headline so big it needed two lines. Beside it, a collection of lanyards — NPR, The Chronicle, Mother Jones, The Intercept — some with photos still attached, faces of reporters who'd probably moved on or moved up or moved out of the business entirely.

The bartender was a woman in her sixties with silver hair pulled back tight and forearms like a dockworker. She glanced at Chloe, at Maya, and went back to wiping the same glass she'd been wiping when they walked in. No carding. No small talk. No acknowledgment that she'd ever seen them before or would again.
"Sit in the back," Chloe said. "Corner booth. Line of sight on the door but no sightline from the street."
"You've done this before."
"Honey, I've done everything before."

They slid into the booth. The vinyl was cracked and cold against Maya's thighs. A single pendant light hung overhead, throwing a cone of amber that made the scarred wood of the table look almost warm. The bar's ambient noise wrapped around them — the low murmur of two old-timers arguing about the A's stadium deal, a Miles Davis track playing from speakers that crackled on the high notes, the occasional clink of glass on glass.

Chloe ordered two club sodas with lime. The lime was a prop — made it look like a drink without the alcohol. Maya wrapped her fingers around the cold glass and let the condensation bead against her skin. Something to hold onto.

"The guy we're meeting," Chloe said, leaning forward so her voice wouldn't carry, "is the best investigative journalist on the West Coast. He's also the most cynical human being I've ever met, and I've met myself."
"Is that supposed to reassure me?"

"It's supposed to prepare you. Jordi doesn't get impressed. He doesn't get scared. He doesn't get sold. He's the most annoying person in any room because he's always two questions ahead of everyone else and he never, ever takes anything at face value." Chloe twisted her lime wedge. "Exactly the kind of person we need."
"And if he says no?"
"Then we find someone else. But there isn't someone else. Not like him."

They waited. Maya watched the door in the reflection of a framed photograph — Robert F. Kennedy on the campaign trail, 1968. The glass was smudged but serviceable as a mirror. Minutes passed. The Miles Davis track ended and something slower took its place. Piano and brushes on a snare, the kind of music that made you feel like you were in a movie even when you were just sitting in a bar in Oakland waiting for a stranger to decide if your story was worth his career.

The door swung open and a man walked in — late twenties, Latino, with the kind of face that looked like it had been assembled from family photos nobody hung on the wall. Sharp jaw, tired eyes, a mouth that defaulted to a flat line. He wore a rumpled charcoal sport coat over a vintage Pixies t-shirt, the Doolittle cover so washed-out Black Francis was practically a ghost. His jeans were dark, his boots were scuffed, and he had a voice recorder visible in his breast pocket — the red recording light wasn't on, but the device sat there like a promise. Trust isn't free. He'd want this on the record or he wouldn't show up at all.
He didn't wave. Didn't scan the room. He walked straight to the booth and sat down across from them, dropping a manila folder on the table without ceremony.
"Chloe Park." He said it like he was reading a file. "The hacktivist who got arrested at the Palantir protest. I covered that story. You threw a pie at Peter Thiel's car."
"It was a very good pie." Chloe's voice was flat, unapologetic. "Local berries. Homemade crust."

"Assault with a delicious weapon. Very on-brand." Jordi didn't smile. He turned to Maya. "And you're the podcaster. Unfollowed. Episode one dropped — what, five days ago? Already got two million downloads and a subreddit full of people who think you're either the next Serial or the next QAnon." He leaned back. "Why are we here?"
Maya pulled out her phone. "I'll let the work speak."

She hit play on Episode 1 and set the phone on the table between them. Her own voice came out of the tiny speaker — strange, hearing herself narrate the thing she'd lived. The breathing pattern segment. The 0.7Hz loop. The smartwatch data. The Lumina blood-type filter. She watched Jordi's face as he listened.

Nothing. Not a twitch. Not a blink that lasted too long. His eyes moved between the phone and the middle distance the way a surgeon looks at an X-ray — reading, not reacting. The bar noise continued around them, indifferent. The old-timers had moved on to the Warriors. The piano on the speakers drifted through a bridge section that sounded like rain on a windshield.
The episode ended. Jordi said nothing.
One second. Three. Five. Maya counted them — a habit she'd picked up measuring the silence between Vlad's breaths on the audio. Seven. Eight. Nine.
Ten.

"The 0.7Hz breathing." He spoke carefully, like he was placing each word on a scale. "I need to verify that independently. The smartwatch data — same. And the Lumina blood-type filter — I'll need to see the backend myself."
Not dismissal. Methodology. Maya felt something loosen in her chest — not relief exactly, but the pressure adjusting. He wasn't laughing them out of the bar.
"I can give you the raw audio files and the data dumps," she said.
"And why should I trust data from an anonymous source?"
Chloe leaned in. Her voice was cool and level. "Because I'm the one who extracted it, and I don't fake evidence. Ever."
Jordi looked at Chloe for a long moment. Then back at Maya. His fingers drummed once on the table — just once — and went still.
"What do you want from me?"

There it was. The question that mattered. The only question that mattered, really. Maya had rehearsed this in her head on the drive over, running through different versions — the emotional pitch, the righteous pitch, the pitch that led with Lucy's name and the threat on her life. She discarded all of them. Jordi Reyes wasn't looking for a cause. He was looking for a story.

"A platform." She kept her voice even. Practical. "My podcast is viral but it's still a podcast. It's audio and algorithms and a subreddit — you said it yourself. When this story breaks — really breaks — it needs to be in The Atlantic. The Times. Somewhere with institutional credibility. Somewhere people can't dismiss with a conspiracy theory tag." She met his eyes. "You have those connections."
"And if I verify and it checks out?"
"Then you get the story of your career." Maya paused. Let it land. "A biotech CEO who's been harvesting women's blood for centuries."
Jordi raised one eyebrow. Just one. A micro-expression so controlled it was practically calligraphy. "Centuries."
"I know how it sounds." Maya held his gaze. "Listen to the breathing pattern again. Then tell me it sounds human."

Jordi didn't answer. He picked up his drink — bourbon, neat, the glass sweating a ring onto the table — and took a long sip. He set it down and reached into the inside pocket of his sport coat. What he pulled out wasn't a phone. It was a notebook — actual paper, Moleskine, the black kind with the elastic band. The pages were dense with handwriting, different colored inks, arrows and brackets and marginalia that looked like a conspiracy theorist's fantasy and a journalist's daily bread.
He uncapped a pen and wrote. Maya watched his hand move across the page: Aeterna. Vlad. Lumina. 847 women. Sublevel B.
He circled Sublevel B. The pen pressed hard enough to dent the page.
Then he looked up, and for the first time since he'd sat down, his eyes met Maya's directly. Not scanning. Not reading. Looking.

"I tried to report on Aeterna six months ago." His voice was different now — not flat, not neutral. There was something underneath, tectonic, shifting. "My editor killed the story. Said it was 'unverifiable claims against a major advertiser.'" He made air quotes with his free hand, the gesture tight and bitter. "Their pharma division spends twelve million a year on ads with us. You do the math."
Maya did. Twelve million dollars. Enough to make a story disappear. Enough to make eight hundred and forty-seven women disappear.
"The system is compromised." Jordi said it like he was finally naming something he'd been choking on for months. "That's why you came to me."
"That's why I came to someone who might still give a damn."

The words hung in the air between them. The bartender put down her glass and picked up another. The Miles Davis was back, something from Kind of Blue, the trumpet line bending like it was trying to hold two notes at once. The old-timers had gone quiet — not listening, just tired. The smell of beer and old newsprint and something that might have been hope or might have been the lemon cleaner failing again pressed in from all sides.

Jordi closed his notebook. He capped the pen with a click that sounded final. He stood, buttoned his sport coat — one button, the middle one, the way men do when they're leaving somewhere they didn't want to be in the first place — and tucked the notebook into his inside pocket.

"Give me seventy-two hours." He wasn't asking. He was telling. "I'll verify the audio and the data independently. If it checks out — and I mean if — I'll write this story. Not for my paper." His jaw tightened. "For everywhere. I'll burn every bridge I have to get this published."
He turned toward the door. Three steps. Then he stopped, hand on the frame, and looked back over his shoulder.

"And Ms. Reyes?" His voice was quiet now, almost gentle, which made it worse. "If this is real — if what you're saying is true — then this isn't just a story. This is a war. And you'd better be ready for that."

He pushed through the door and was gone. The door swung once, twice, settled. The amber light still hung over the empty booth across from them, illuminating the ring of condensation where his glass had been.
Maya exhaled. She hadn't realized she'd been holding her breath.
Chloe swirled her club soda. The lime had sunk to the bottom like a tiny green submarine.
"Told you," she said. "Cynical."

But there was something in Chloe's eyes — a spark, small and bright and stubborn, the kind of light that doesn't show up in a room until someone strikes a match. Maya saw it because she felt the same thing in her own chest. A warmth. Not comfort exactly. Something sharper. Something that felt like the first time she'd hit publish on the podcast — the terror and the certainty braided together into a rope she could climb.
They had an army now. Small. Three people and a dead man's breathing pattern and a notebook with a circle around the words Sublevel B. But an army nonetheless.
Maya raised her glass. Chloe raised hers. They clinked — a sound so quiet it barely registered over the trumpet filling the bar, a note that bent and held and refused to resolve.
They drank. The ice settled.
Seventy-two hours. Twenty-two days to IPO.
The war was coming.
